channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - wccftech.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for wccftech.com

item.title: PS6 Handheld Will Reportedly Beat the Xbox Series S In Both Raster and Ray Tracing; PSSR 3 Will Outperform NVIDIA DLSS 4.5
item.content:encoded:
• 0-20%: Unlikely - Lacks credible sources
  • 21-40%: Questionable - Some concerns remain
  • 41-60%: Plausible - Reasonable evidence
  • 61-80%: Probable - Strong evidence
  • 81-100%: Highly Likely - Multiple reliable sources

Sony’s return to the handheld market with the PS6 Handheld is set to be very interesting as, according to known AMD leaker KeplerL2, the system will outperform the Xbox Series S in both rasterization and ray tracing. Furthermore, it is expected to deliver superior image quality with PSSR 3 over what the Nintendo Switch 2 can achieve with its NVIDIA DLSS "Light".

"GPU is a bit ahead of XSS in raster (and ofc massively ahead in RT/PT)," said the leaker on the NeoGAF forums while discussing the leaked specifications of the system currently known as "Project Canis." The leaked specifications in question are:

  • 4 x Zen 6c Cores
  • 2 x Zen 6 LP Cores
  • 16 x RDNA 5 CUs
  • 192-bit LPDDR5X (24GB)
  • 135mm² TSMC 3nm

Needless to say, this early performance comparison sounds incredibly promising. With the Xbox Series S having been the baseline for games during the current generation before the launch of the Nintendo Switch 2 last year, the PS6 Handheld is set to deliver a high-end on-the-go gaming experience. It is an experience that, in terms of image quality, is set to look significantly better than what the Nintendo Switch 2 offers, and not only because of the significantly better specs.

"Switch 2 only has DLSS 2 (CNN) and some games even use the worse 'DLSS Lite.' FSR5/PSSR3 will be better IQ than even current DLSS 4.5," continued KeplerL2, highlighting how the next version of Sony’s AI-powered upscaler could be the one feature that elevates the PS6 Handheld. This would place it above not only Nintendo’s current system, which is currently the only dedicated console handheld supporting AI-upscaling, but also other current handheld gaming systems, such as the Xbox ROG Ally X, which rely on other upscaling solutions. Seeing how much PSSR improved in version 2.0, it will definitely be interesting to see it in action, especially on a portable device.

Amidst the price hikes of components, which could make next-generation hardware such as the PlayStation 6 and Xbox Project Helix very expensive (so much so that Michael Pachter believes game streaming could be the only option for most), the PS6 Handheld could be Sony’s best idea for the next generation. A system priced at the $399 point could make users more inclined to upgrade than a more expensive home system even at the $699 price point, according to Alderon Games' Matthew Cassells.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/ps6-handheld-beat-xbox-series-s-pssr-3/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: GTA 6 Developer Has Spent Nearly $3 Billion on Staff Costs Alone Since 2019, and That’s Just Rockstar North
item.content:encoded:
There is little doubt that GTA 6 is going to be the most expensive game ever made, but just how expensive? Well, a Redditor looked into Rockstar North's public-facing documents, available on a UK government website, and found its officially disclosed staff expenses.

GTA 6 started early development in late 2018, following Rockstar's most recent game release (Red Dead Redemption 2). Over the years 2019-2025, Rockstar North spent nearly $3 billion on total staff costs alone, including wages and salaries, social security, pension costs, and share-based payments.

On the one hand, Rockstar North did not just work on GTA 6 during all this time; the developer is still supporting the popular GTA Online and (to a much lesser extent since July 2022) even Red Dead Online. On the other hand, while staff costs certainly make up the majority of triple-A game development costs in countries like the US, Canada, and the UK, there are other costs as well. Furthermore, while the Edinburgh-based Rockstar North is certainly Rockstar's heart, there are many other offices scattered across the globe, including San Diego, Toronto, New England, Lincoln, Leeds, London, Dundee, Sydney, and Bengaluru (Rockstar India).

When adding that the studio's development approach is to give developers a virtually unlimited blueprint, it's far from a stretch to imagine that the overall GTA 6 budget, when considering all development costs and the soon-to-begin massive marketing campaign, could reach $4 or even 5 billion, making it by far the most expensive entertainment product ever made. For comparison, Bloomberg's Jason Schreier recently revealed that most triple-A games cost around $300 million nowadays.

Rockstar is in a whole other ballpark compared to anything else and will most likely recoup its expenses within days of the launch of GTA 6, given the incredible hype surrounding the release, now set for November 19, 2026 (barring any further delays). If GTA Online is any indication, the bulk of profits will likely come from its online mode, which, according to rumors we've previously gathered from an industry veteran, may be MMORPG-like.

As a reminder, Rockstar North recently faced controversy after firing several employees for leaking sensitive information. However, the employees countered that no information was actually leaked and that the terminations were specifically targeted at Rockstar employees who were on the verge of unionizing. A few employees were also laid off at the Toronto-based office, and our David Carcasole interviewed them after an on-site protest outside the studio's Canadian headquarters.

Rockstar North was also in the news earlier this year due to an explosion in a boiler room. Luckily, the accident did not leave any employees injured.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/gta-6-developer-spent-nearly-3-billion-staff-costs-alone/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Eidos Montréal Reportedly Canceled a Game Codenamed Wildlands That Cost Hundreds of Millions of Dollars
item.content:encoded:
• 0-20%: Unlikely - Lacks credible sources
  • 21-40%: Questionable - Some concerns remain
  • 41-60%: Plausible - Reasonable evidence
  • 61-80%: Probable - Strong evidence
  • 81-100%: Highly Likely - Multiple reliable sources

This week, Canadian developer Eidos Montréal suffered another major round of layoffs, cutting 124 jobs and losing its long-time Studio Head, David Anfossi. Shortly after the news broke, Jason Schreier added that it was related to a game cancellation, and now Insider Gaming has provided more color on that project.

This was the open world action/adventure game that, according to Tom Henderson, was supposed to launch in 2026. Codenamed Wildlands, it began development in 2019, before the global pandemic, and underwent four game engine changes, as well as major internal conflicts over its narrative direction.

As a result of all these issues, the game's development had already cost hundreds of millions of dollars. Yet, it was reportedly almost complete and was in the "debugging phase", according to Henderson. The insider speculates the decision to cancel it came from the parent company Embracer Group rather than Eidos Montréal itself.

The report even shares a little bit about the game. Wildlands was an open world action/adventure game played in third-person, in which players took control of a character named River. River belonged to a group of teenagers called the Spiritbounds who could fend off evil spirits with their magical staffs and ride mythical creatures. The protagonist's companion was a giant moose-like animal called Redheart, which River could ride to navigate the map.

Unfortunately, this is just the latest cancellation for Eidos Montréal. The studio previously cancelled a new Legacy of Kain game, a new Deus Ex game, and a modern point-and-click game with turn-based combat. To this day, its most recent game release is Marvel's Guardians of the Galaxy, which launched in October 2021 and, despite being a very good game, did not sell very well.

Eidos Montréal also helped Playground Games with their Fable reboot, which must have given them some financial security, but their involvement in the soon-to-be-released game reportedly ended over a year ago. Hopefully, their next project will have better luck; we've seen far too many historic game development studios being shut down lately.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/eidos-montreal-canceled-game-wildlands-hundreds-millions/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
